coredata.dc:title: Reuse of waste Myrica rubra for green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots as an “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for Fe3+ and ascorbic acid detection

coredata.dc:description: As one kind of high nutrition fruits, abandoned Myrica rubra causes great waste due to short storage period. For resource utilization, we herein fabricated the Myrica rubra-based N-doped carbon dots (MN-CDs) by a facile/green hydrothermal method. MN-CDs, fabricated from four regions of China, displayed significant differences in their corresponding fluorescence intensities (FIs). Interestingly, different batches of waxberry samples from the same region (Wenzhou, China) exhibited slight differences in their FIs, and also an excellent anti-photobleaching and anti-salt capacity. Based on Fe3+-triggered quenching effect and fluorescent recovery by redox reaction of AA and Fe3+, MN-CDs were employed to construct an “on-off-on” switch probe for sequential detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid (AA). Through Zeta potential, UV spectrum, Stern-Volmer equation, and valence-conduction band theory, the Fe3+-triggered quenching belonged to a static quenching process, which resulted from the synergistic contribution of inner filtering effect and photo-induced electron transfer mechanisms. The linear ranges for Fe3+ and AA detections were 1–1000 and 0.1–1000 mM. The limits of detection were 0.3 μM for Fe3+ in environmental waters, and 0.03 μM for AA in pharmaceutical tablets and fruit juice samples. Under 365-nm UV lamp, the color changes of test papers were easily observed from dark blue and bright blue in the presence of Fe3+ and AA, and thus the MN-CDs-based switch probe could be satisfactorily used for visually qualitative detection of Fe3+ and AA outdoor with our naked eyes. To sum up, MN-CDs not only realize resource reutilization of abandoned Myrica rubra, but also offer an convenient outdoor approach for qualitative detection of Fe3+ and AA in complex matrices.

head.ce:title: Reuse of waste Myrica rubra for green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots as an “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for Fe3+ and ascorbic acid detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As one kind of high nutrition fruits, abandoned Myrica rubra causes great waste due to short storage period. For resource utilization, we herein fabricated the Myrica rubra-based N-doped carbon dots (MN-CDs) by a facile/green hydrothermal method. MN-CDs, fabricated from four regions of China, displayed significant differences in their corresponding fluorescence intensities (FIs). Interestingly, different batches of waxberry samples from the same region (Wenzhou, China) exhibited slight differences in their FIs, and also an excellent anti-photobleaching and anti-salt capacity. Based on Fe3+-triggered quenching effect and fluorescent recovery by redox reaction of AA and Fe3+, MN-CDs were employed to construct an “on-off-on” switch probe for sequential detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid (AA). Through Zeta potential, UV spectrum, Stern-Volmer equation, and valence-conduction band theory, the Fe3+-triggered quenching belonged to a static quenching process, which resulted from the synergistic contribution of inner filtering effect and photo-induced electron transfer mechanisms. The linear ranges for Fe3+ and AA detections were 1–1000 and 0.1–1000 mM. The limits of detection were 0.3 μM for Fe3+ in environmental waters, and 0.03 μM for AA in pharmaceutical tablets and fruit juice samples. Under 365-nm UV lamp, the color changes of test papers were easily observed from dark blue and bright blue in the presence of Fe3+ and AA, and thus the MN-CDs-based switch probe could be satisfactorily used for visually qualitative detection of Fe3+ and AA outdoor with our naked eyes. To sum up, MN-CDs not only realize resource reutilization of abandoned Myrica rubra, but also offer an convenient outdoor approach for qualitative detection of Fe3+ and AA in complex matrices.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to advantageous nanostructures and optical properties, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely developed for biosensors, drug delivery, and fluorescence detection (Lv et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). The fabrication of CDs can be realized by electrochemical, hydrothermal, ultrasonic, and microwave treatments (Rajabi et al., 2017, 2020; Niu et al., 2018; Shamsipur et al., 2018; Moradi Alvand et al., 2019; Alvand et al., 2021). Natural biomass materials are common carbon sources for a facile preparation of CDs, which can effectively avoid the employment of expensive and toxic chemicals and complex post-treatment procedures (Ye et al., 2020). Myrica rubra is a subtropical fruit from South China that is popular for its unique flavor and high nutritional value (Dai et al., 2021). In China, the annual output of Myrica rubra is ~300,000 tons, and its fruit is usually processed into juice with high commercial value (Zhang et al., 2018). However, Myrica rubra fruit is highly perishable and only lasts for about 1–2 days at room temperature, making it extremely wasteful (Ma et al., 2016). Therefore, it is urgent to extend the storage time after harvesting or to reutilize it as a biological resource. In recent years, natural biomass materials, such as flax straw (Hu et al., 2020), lemon juice (Tadesse et al., 2020), and bamboo leaves (Yang et al., 2020), have been successfully adopted for synthesis of the fluorescent CDs. However, no report is involed in the fabrication of CDs from Myrica rubra as a kind of natural carbon source. The red color of Myrica rubra is originated from the higher anthocyanins content than other fruits and vegetables (Bai et al., 2005). Theoretically, we confer that the fluorescent intensity of Myrica rubra-based CDs should be stronger than other CDs, probably because of rich heterocyclic structures of anthocyanins. Meanwhile, the development of Myrica rubra as fluorescent CDs can realize the resource reutilization of waste biomass.

Ascorbic acid (AA), known as vitamin C, is widely existed in fresh fruits and plays a vital role in human growth, metabolism, and development (Rao et al., 2017; Song et al., 2019). However, an excessive intake of AA will result in urinary stone, diarrhea and stomach convulsion (Li et al., 2020). Consequently, the development of quantitative probe for AA assay is very important for early prevention and point of care diagnosis of these diseases.

As an essential metal ion, Fe3+ plays a vital role in many pathological processes and natural environment (Liu et al., 2017). Abnormal Fe3+ level in human body can lead to physiological damage, such as renal failure, anemia, liver injury, and heart disease (Hu et al., 2019). Additionally, iron pollution seriously threatens environmental safety; according to the China national standard (GB 5749–2006), the Fe content in drinking water should not exceed 0.3 mg/L. Among a large number of reported methods, fluorescence analysis has captured a lot of interest because of its simplicity, rapidity and high sensitivity for detecting Fe3+ and AA (Na et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2019b). Many types of fluorescent sensors has been developed for Fe3+ detection, such as N-doped CDs (Lv et al., 2020), CQDs (Zhang et al., 2020), tea-CDs (Chen et al., 2019), N-CQDs (Niu et al., 2018), C-dots (Wang et al., 2020), and N,S-CDs (Yang et al., 2020).

Currently, the elemental doping with effective approaches, such as doped with Fe, Mn, N, B, and P, is widely used to adjust the surface state, optics, and structural characteristics of nanomaterials (Shamsipur et al., 2013; Rajabi and Farsi, 2015, 2016). Especially, the co-doping of multielements can significantly improve the selectivity, sensitivity, photocatalytic performance, excitation and emission behavior, as well as photon transfer efficiency of nanoprobes (Rajabi et al., 2013, 2016, 2017; Shamsipur and Rajabi, 2014; Hemmateenejad et al., 2015). For the resource utilization of abandoned Myrica rubra in South China, we employed waxberry to synthesize, for the first time, the N-doped CDs by a facile hydrothermal method. Hereafter, the Myrica rubra based N-doped CDs were abbreviated as MN-CDs. A series of characterizations demonstrated that the MN-CDs were doped with N atoms and had rich functional groups on the surface, thereby possessing strong inner fluorescence and good chemical stability. The fluorescence intensities (FIs) of MN-CDs were sharply quenched upon addition of Fe3+; however, on the basis of redox reaction, they were quickly recovered after adding AA ( Scheme 1). As a consequence, we constructed an "on-off-on" fluorescence switch sensor for sequential assay of Fe3+ and AA, and developed a paper-based sensing platform for their rapid and convenient visualization detection outdoors, which shows great application prospects in environmental/food monitoring and pharmaceutical analysis.

All chemicals utilized in this investigation were of analytical grade or better and used when received without further purification. All stock and work solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (>18.2 MΩ) from a Milli-Q gradient system (Bedford, MA, USA). The following chemicals/reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Corporation (Shanghai, China): CuSO4.5 H2O, Fe2(SO4)3, FeSO4, MnCl2, Ca(NO3)2, ZnCl2, Al(NO3)3•9 H2O, CoCl2•6 H2O, Ni(CH3COO)2•4 H2O, Mg(NO3)2•6 H2O, Ce(NO3)2•6 H2O and CdCl2. A series of amino acids and carbohydrate compounds with purity of 99.0% were gratis supplied by Aladdin (Shanghai, China): ascorbic acid (AA), glutathione (GSH), L-cysteine (L-cys), glucose (Glu), citric acid (CA), histidine (His), glycine (Gly), serine (Ser), lysine (Lys), proline (Pro), threonine (Thr), alanine (Ala), sucrose (Suc) and fructose (Fru).

The waxberry and fruit juice samples were acquired from a local fruit supermarket (Wenzhou, China). Vitamin C tablets were purchased from a local pharmacy in Wenzhou, China. The tap and barreled drinking water samples were collected from our laboratory of Wenzhou Medical University, Wenzhou, China. The river water was collected from Shunao Branch of Wenruitang River, Wenzhou, China. Both the dialysis bag (MWCO=2000 Dalton) and 0.22-μm polyether sulfone (PES) membrane filters were obtained from Aladdin.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were determined using a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Billerica, USA), TVERTEX-70 (Bruker, Germany), and Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), respectively. Raman spectrum was analyzed using a HORIBA (Japan). Fluorescent emission spectrum was recorded on a FS5 Spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments, England). Zeta potential was determined by a Malvern ZEN3690 Zetasizer (Malvern, USA). TEM image of the MN-CDs was taken on a TALOS F200S (FEI, USA). UV–vis absorption was carried out on a PERSEE TU-1901 (Pgeneral, China).

MN-CDs were prepared using a facile hydrothermal method as described by Luo and coworkers with minor modifications (Luo et al., 2019). First, an aliquot (5 g) of the waxberry sample was crushed and transferred to a 100 mL container. Then, 25 mL ultrapure water was added, and the mixed solution were stirred homogeneously and transferred to a 100-mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was placed into an oven at 190 °C for 11 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the solution of MN-CDs was filtered with a 0.22-μm PES membrane filter. The as-prepared solution was stored at 4 ℃ for later use.

Quinine sulfate was adopted as a standard reference, i.e., at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, its QY was 54% in 0.1 M of H2SO4 solution (Yang et al., 2020). To minimize the interfering effect, the absorbance of quinine sulfate and MN-CDs were kept under 0.1 at the 360-nm excitation wavelength (Liu et al., 2017). The FIs and UV–vis absorption spectra of quinine sulfate were detected under the same conditions (Hu et al., 2020). The QYs of MN-CDs were calculated based on the Eq. (1):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of Fe3+ and AA
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical process, 100 μL of the MN-CDs solution was dispersed homogeneously in Tris-HCl buffer (20 mM, pH=7.4), in which 100 μL of the Fe3+ solutions at varying concentrations were added, and the final solution volume was made up to 1.0 mL. The FIs of solutions were detected immediately after 2-min incubation. As for the assay of AA, 100 μL of the MN-CDs and 100 μL of the Fe3+ solution (2.5 mM) were mixed homogeneously in Tris-HCl buffer (20 mM, pH=7.4). After a 2-min incubation, the resultant MN-CDs/Fe3+ system was added by the solutions of AA at varying concentrations, and the final volume of the mixed solution was supplemented to 1.0 mL. After 10-min reaction, the FIs of the aforementioned system were recorded to investigate the recovered fluorescence at ambient conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of Fe3+ and AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For the sake of evaluating the feasibility of as-fabricated “on-off-on” sensor, the standard fortifying approach was employed to detect Fe3+ and AA in environmental water, pharmaceutical tablet, and fruit juice samples. The collected water samples were filtered through 0.45-µm syringe filters to eliminate the suspended solids. In the pretreatment of VC tablet, a tablet was mixed with 20 mL of 0.2 g/L oxalic acid, after 20-min ultrasound, the resultant solution was filtered through a 0.45-µm syringe filter to remove solid suspensions. Subsequently, the filtrate was diluted using 0.2 g/L of oxalic acid for further use. In aspects of pretreatment for the fruit samples, an aliquot (30 g) of the pulp was mixed with 50 mL of 0.2 g/L oxalic acid, and then crushed by a juicer. After 10-min centrifugation at 8000 rpm, the solution was also filtered and diluted for later use. Three gradient fortification concentrations of Fe3+ were 10, 20, and 40 µM in waters. In comparison, three spiking concentrations of AA were 50, 100, and 150 µM in vitamin C tablets, and 5, 10, and 20 µM in fruit juices samples, respectively.

The common filter paper was purchased from Shanghai Xingya Purification Material Factory (Shanghai, China), which was cut into many circles with a diameter of 1.0 cm. For each circular paper, 100 μL of the MN-CDs solution was dropped slowly/homogeneously to ensure covering the whole surface of test paper. After the test paper was dried at 40 ℃, the solutions containing the different concentrations of Fe3+ were dripped on the surface of the as-fabricated test papers to observe the color changes under UV lamp irradiation. For the detection of AA, the mixed solution of MN-CDs (100 μL) and Fe3+ solution (100 μL, 2.5 mM) were added dropwise to the surface of test paper; after 2-min incubation, the above pretreated samples were dripped to watch the color changes under UV lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the MN-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: According to the method described in Section 2.3, the Myrica rubra samples were employed to prepare the MN-CDs by a facile hydrothermal method. In the synthetic process, reaction temperature and time were optimized in detail, which spanned over the range of 160–200 °C and 6–12 h, respectively. As exhibited in Fig. S1a and b, the highest FIs were observed at 190 °C for 11 h, and the further increases in synthetic temperatures and times would lead to the prominently decreased FIs of MN-CDs. Consequently, the above reaction conditions were adopted for the subsequent preparation of MN-CDs. At the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, the as-fabricated MN-CDs emitted strong blue-green fluorescence at 440 nm, as vividly elaborated in Scheme 1.

Based on TEM observation, the morphology and particle size distribution of as-fabricated MN-CDs are shown in Fig. 1a and b. Obviously, they displayed spherical dots and were evenly distributed with the size range of 1.72–4.58 nm (an average diameter of ~3.15 nm, Fig. 1b). The XRD patterns displayed a strong diffraction peak at 2θ = 29° and a weak peak at 2θ = 42°, which corresponded to the (002) and (101) diffraction planes of graphite carbon, respectively (Fig. 1c). The wide diffraction peaks confirmed the amorphous nature of carbonaceous nanomaterials (Tadesse et al., 2020; Ye et al., 2020). The Raman spectrum of MN-CDs exhibited two peaks at 1359 and 1596 cm−1 (Fig. 1d), which correspond to the disordered D band and the graphitic G band, respectively (Qiang et al., 2019). The ratio of ID/IG was calculated to be 0.87, demonstrating that the coexistence of sp2 graphitic carbon and sp3 carbon defects in the as-fabricated MN-CDs (Guo et al., 2019a).

As for the FT-IR spectra of MN-CDs, the wide band in the range of 3236–3450 cm−1 resulted from the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H, while the narrow peak at 2930 cm−1 was attributed to the tensile vibration of C-H (Hu et al., 2020) ( Fig. 2a). Besides, the band at 1521 cm−1 could be assigned to the tensile vibration of CC (Shukla et al., 2020). The absorption peaks at 1320 and 1410 cm−1 belonged to the contribution of C-N stretching vibrations and in-plane of amide-carbonyl (Xue et al., 2018; Qu et al., 2020). Moreover, three characteristic peaks at 1703, 1165 and 1025 cm−1 were originated from the CO, C-O-C and C-O bonds, respectively (Xue et al., 2018).

The XPS survey spectra were employed to analyze the surface composition and chemical structure of MN-CDs. Three strong peaks of 285.1, 400.1 and 558.2 eV were attributed to C1 s, N1 s and O1 s, respectively (Fig. 2b), demonstrating the presence of C, N and O atoms in the MN-CDs. In the high-resolution C1 s spectrum of MN-CDs (Fig. 2c), the peak at 284.8 eV was corresponding to sp2 C (C-C or CC) (Zhao et al., 2019), while those at 285.7 and 288.1 eV were assigned to the C−N/C-O and CO, respectively (Guo et al., 2019a; Qiang et al., 2019). The deconvolution analysis of N1 s spectrum suggested the existence of pyrrolic N (399.9 eV) and pyridinic N (401.5 eV) (Edison et al., 2016; Bandi et al., 2019), as exhibited in Fig. 2d. The high-resolution XPS spectra of O1 s were deconvoluted into three peaks at 531.8, 532.9 and 533.5 eV, which resulted from the *OC-O, C-OH/C-O-C, H-O-H bonds, respectively (Fig. S1c) (Bandi et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2020). These data provide compelling evidence that the MN-CDs were doped with N atoms and an abundance of functional groups existed on the surface of MN-CDs.

By UV–vis spectral analyses, the 284-nm absorption peak was corresponding to the n-π * transition of CO bond (Fig. S2a) (Shen et al., 2021), and the solution of MN-CDs showed light yellow under daylight (inset of Fig. S2a). At the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, the maximum emission wavelength occurred at 440 nm (Fig. S2b), and under 365-nm UV irradiation, the MN-CDs solution showed bright blue (inset of Fig. S2b). The fluorescence spectra of MN-CDs are displayed in Fig. S2c across the excitation wavelength range of 320–420 nm. Significantly, the lower and higher wavelengths than 350 nm led to a certain amount of decrease in the FIs. According to Eq. (1), the linear slope ratio of quinine sulfate to MN-CDs was equal to the ratio of QYs, and the absorbance of both quinine sulfate and MN-CDs was controlled to be < 0.1. The obtained results are exhibited in Fig. S3a and b and generalized in Table S1. As a result, the QY of MN-CDs was computed to be ~23.4%.

Additionally, the effects of several key factors on the stability of MN-CDs were investigated in detail, including salt concentration, UV irradiation and storage durability, as well as different batches and types of Myrica rubra samples. As can be seen from Fig. S2d, the different concentrations of NaCl (0–1.0 M) had slight effects on the FIs of MN-CDs, reflecting in that the quenching efficiency was < 6.7% even at the highest fortification concentration (1.0 M). The corresponding fluorescence spectra in the presence of NaCl are elaborated in Fig. S1d, and the slight differences in FIs confirm that the MN-CDs possess strong stability in high salt environment. Similarly, the fluorescence of MN-CDs varied < 7.2% after 60-min UV irradiation (Fig. S2e and Fig. S4a), implying an excellent anti-photobleaching capability. After 7 days of storage, the quenched FIs of MN-CDs were only ~7.7% (Fig. S2f and Fig. S4b), demonstrating the fluorescent stability with at least one week. Thus, the as-fabricated nanomaterials within 7 days were employed to investigate their FIs for the sake of reducing experimental errors. Moreover, by utilizing the same species of Wenzhou Chashan waxberry, four batches of fresh samples were collected in May 18, 20, 22 and 24, 2021, respectively, to probe their effects on the fluorescence of as-fabricated MN-CDs. It is obvious in Fig. S4c that the different batches had slight effects on the FIs of MN-CDs, with the relative standard deviations of < 5.4%. By way of contrast, four species of Myrica rubra samples, obtained from Wenzhou, Zhangzhou, Suzhou and Taizhou, led to the significant differences in their corresponding fluorescence signals (Fig. S4d). The above phenomenon may be explained by that owing to the great differences in regional climate and soil, different types and contents of atoms are possibly doped on the surface of MN-CDs, thereby resulting in differential functional groups and FIs. Overall, these findings demonstrate that the as-fabricated MN-CDs have strong fluorescence stability and thus show wide application prospects in analytical field.

To acquire the best experimental conditions, the reaction time, reaction concentration, and pH of buffer solution were optimized. In the solution pH range of 3.4–8.4, the FIs of MN-CDs increased gradually from 3.4 to 7.4, but a sharply decreasing trend was observed from 7.4 to 8.4 because of strong alkalinity of the solution (Fig. S5a). As a result, the pH of buffer solution was set at 7.4 in subsequent experiments. Meanwhile, upon addition of Fe3+, the highest quenching efficiency up to 73.3% was also found at pH 7.4 (Fig. S5b). As a result, the pH of buffer solution was set at 7.4 in the following trials.

In the construction of an “on-off-on” sensor, the proper amount of quenching agent (Fe3+ in this investigation) is very important. As depicted in Fig. S5c, the quenching efficiency increased gradually with the increasing Fe3+ concentrations from 1.0 to 2.0 mM, but they changed slightly with further increases from 2.0 to 3.0 mM. Besides, when adding AA in the MN-CDs/Fe3+ system, the strongest fluorescence recovery was observed after adding 2.5 mM Fe3+ (Fig. S5d), but an excessive amount of Fe3+ led to chemical reversible reactions with AA, further causing the reduced recovery efficiency. Overall, 2.5 mM Fe3+ could not only produce satisfactory quenching effect, but also cause an appropriate recovery efficiency. So, it was chosen as the optimal Fe3+ concentration.

The effect of incubation time on quenching efficiency is shown in Fig. S6a. When 100 μL Fe3+ (1.5 mM) was added to the MN-CDs solution, the quenching efficiency increased rapidly to 83.0% in the first 2 min and remained nearly unchanged from 2 to 10 min. Similarly, when 100 μL AA (1.5 mM) was added to the MN-CDs/Fe3+ system, the percentages of fluorescent recovery reached as high as 41.0% after 10-min incubation and remained unchanged thereafter (Fig. S6b). Therefore, the best incubation time for Fe3+ and AA was adopted to be 2 and 10 min, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fe3+ and AA by the MN-CDs probe
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of as-constructed "on-off-on" platform was tested under optimized conditions. The FIs of MN-CDs decreased prominently with the increasing Fe3+ concentrations (0–1.0 mM) ( Fig. 3a), and a plot of the F/F0 against the concentration of Fe3+ was constructed with coefficient of determination (R2) as high as 0.9998 (Fig. 3b). Based on the signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (S/N = 3), the limit of detection (LOD) was computed to be 0.3 μM and the linear range (LR) spanned over the range of 1.0–1000 μM.

Moreover, the fluorescent reversibility of the MN-CDs/Fe3+ system was investigated in the presence of AA. As vividly illustrated in Fig. 3c, the quenched FIs by Fe3+ was recovered immediately upon addition of AA, which showed a strong concentration-dependence. Namely, the FIs of the /Fe3+ system gradually recovered with rises in the fortification concentrations of AA. Building upon the spectral data, the LR for the calibration curve between F1/F and AA was in the range of 0.1–1000 μM with R2 of 0.9988 (Fig. 3d), and the resultant LOD was 0.03 μM. In contrast to the previously reported sensors, this MN-CDs-based sensor possesses lower or comparable LOD and wider LR for AA assay (Rao et al., 2017; Guo et al., 2019b; Song et al., 2019).

This proposed “on-off-on” sensor was compared with other approaches reported previously in terms of LRs and LODs for the detection of Fe3+ and AA. As listed in Table S2, the LOD (0.3 μM) of Fe3+ offered by this sensor is ~2–10-fold lower than those of NCDs (0.7 μM) (Li et al., 2021), N-doped CDs (1.2 μM) (Lv et al., 2020), CQDs (2.8 μM) (Zhang et al., 2020), and DPYBT (3.0 μM) (Qian et al., 2020). Better yet, the LR (0.1–1000 μM for Fe3+ and 1–1000 μM for AA) is much wider as compared to these mentioned approaches. In aspects of LOD for AA detection, the MN-CDs/Fe3+ sensing platform is superior or comparable to the previously reported methods, such as CQDs/Fe3+ (Zhang et al., 2020), RhB@DiCH3MOF-5/Fe3+ (Guo et al., 2019b), S,N-CDs/Cr 6+ (Song et al., 2019), and Cu-ZnCdS QDs/MnO2 (Rao et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interference effect of the MN-CDs-based probe for analyzing Fe3+ and AA
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-interference effect of as-fabricated probe is one of the important parameters in determining its feasibility in real detection. Herein, a series of metal cations were added to the solutions of MN-CDs, including Cu2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Ce3+, Ca2+ and K+, at a fortification concentration of 10 mM (Fig. S7a). The investigated anions consisted of F−1, SO4 2-, CO3 2-, Ac−1, C2O4 2-, HCO3-, H2PO4-, PO4 3- and S2O8 2- as same as the fortified concentrations of anions (Fig. S6c). Clearly, only adding Fe3+ (1.5 mM) to the MN-CDs solution led to an obviously quenching phenomenon; under 365-nm UV irradiation, the original bright blue changed to dark grey in the solutions. However, no obvious color changes occurred in the presence of all interfering cations and anions, i.e., showing the original bright blue (insets of Fig. S7a and Fig. S7c).

Moreover, the selectivity of the MN-CDs/Fe3+ system to AA was investigated by adding a series of biological macromolucules at 10 mM, which included glutathione (GSH), L-cysteine (L-cys), glucose (Glu), citric acid (CA), histidine (His), glycine (Gly), serine (Ser), proline (Pro), alanine (Ala), threonine (Thr), sucrose (Suc) and fructose (Fru). It can be seen from Fig. S6b that only the addition of AA led to the changes of solution color from dark grey to bright blue, followed by the slight color changes by the addition of GSH and Cys (inset of Fig. S7b). In sharp contrast, other 10 macromolecules did not cause any color changes, evidencing the high selectivity of as-developed “on-off” probe to AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The underlying mechanisms regarding the MN-CDs-based probe
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S8a, the MN-CDs emitted bright blue fluorescence at 440 nm, and it was worth noting that the addition of AA had not any effects on the FIs in the absence of Fe3+. However, the FIs were dramatically reduced after adding Fe3+, and then recovered upon addition of AA.

The fluorescence quenching can be described by the Stern-Volmer Eq. (2) (Li et al., 2021):
(2)

K

K

↊

[ Q ]
where F0 and F are the FIs before and after adding quencher, respectively; Ksv (L mol−1) is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant; [Q] is the quencher concentration; Kq (L mol−1 s−1) is the bimolecular quenching constant; and τ0 is the average lifetime of fluorescent molecules in the absence of quencher, with a general value of 10−8 s (Yang et al., 2020). Generally speaking, the static quenching constant decreases with the increase of temperature, while the dynamic quenching constant increases with the increase of system temperature (Zhao et al., 2018). Over the temperature range of 293–313 K, the Ksv values decreased with the rising temperatures (Fig. S8b and Table S3), implying that the quenching process induced by Fe3+ belonged to a static quenching mechanism.

The time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra of MN-CDs are illustrated in Fig. 4a and the related parameters are listed in Table S4 in the presence and absence of Fe3+ (500 μM). By the double exponential decay functions, the computed fluorescence lifetimes were 3.0 and 2.8 ns, respectively, in the presence/absence of Fe3+, highlighting a slight variation. The UV absorption spectra are shown in Fig. S8c in the Fe3+, MN-CDs, MN-CDs/Fe3+, and MN-CDs/Fe3+/AA systems. Obviously, the absorption spectrum of MN-CDs/Fe3+ was different from those of MN-CDs and Fe3+, highlighting that Fe3+ interacted with the surface groups of MN-CDs to constitute the MN-CDs/Fe3+ complexes. The excited electrons of MN-CDs were transferred to the half-filled 3d orbitals of Fe3+, causing fluorescence quenching by non-radiation electron-hole annihilation (Gao et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2019). In the process of fluorescence recovery, the absorption spectrum of MN-CDs/Fe3+/AA suggested that after Fe3+ was reduced by AA, the complexes were decomposed to recover the inner fluorescence of MN-CDs. These spectral observations confirm that the underlying quenching mechanisms resulted from a static quenching process.

As indicated in Fig. S8d, the Zeta potential of the MN-CDs solution was − 7.1 mV; in sharp contrast, that of the MN-CDs/Fe3+ system was 2.5 mV. The distinct difference in their Zeta potentials hinted occurrence of a coordination reaction between Fe3+ and the surface groups of MN-CDs. The previous reports demonstrated that the Fe3+-triggered quenching effect was mainly attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE) or photo-induced electron transfer (PET) mechanism (Guo et al., 2018, 2019b). Fe3+ had a strong absorption in the wavelength range of 250–400 nm (Fig. 4b), which was overlapped with the 350-nm excitation peak of MN-CDs. This spectral overlapping phenomenon dropped a hint that the fluorescence of MN-CDs was quenched by Fe3+ due to IFE mechanism (Li et al., 2018). According to the geometry of cuvette used in fluorescent measurement (inset of Fig. 4c), IFE between MN-CDs and Fe3+ was corrected by the Eq. (3) (Zhu et al., 2015)

where CF is the revised coefficient; Fobsd and Fcor represent the uncorrected FIs of MN-CDs and corrected FIs by removal of IFE from Fobsd, respectively (Zhai et al., 2014); Aex and Aem denote the absorbance per centimeter of MN-CDs at the excitation and emission wavelengths.

Fig. 4c shows the fluorescence quenching efficiency of observed (Qobsd) and corrected (Qcor) of MN-CDs at varying concentrations of Fe3+. The detailed calculation process and data on the important parameters are summarized in Table S5. At the fortification concentration of 400 μM, the Qobsd value (total quenching efficiency) was ~43.4%, while the Qcor value (IFE mechanism) was ~13.2%. As a result, the contribution percentage of IFE mechanism to total quenching efficiency accounted for ~30.4%. These data provide convincing evidence that the Fe3+-triggered quenching of MN-CDs was not only due to IFE mechanism, but also affected by other mechanisms.

According to the UV–vis absorption spectrum, the electron band-gap (Eg) of MN-CDs was calculated to be 1.9 eV by the Tauc plot method (Fig. 4d) (Bai et al., 2020). Through the XPS valence-band spectrum, the valence band (VB) of MN-CDs was 1.7 eV vs. normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) (Fig. 4e) (Guo et al., 2018). By means of the formula: ECB = EVB – Eg, the conduction band (CB) was computed to be − 0.3 eV vs. NHE (Bai et al., 2020). The standard electrode potential of Fe3+/Fe2+ was 0.8 eV vs. NHE, which was located approximately in the middle of CB (−0.3 eV) and VB (1.7 eV) of MN-CDs. When the MN-CDs were excited by UV irradiation, the electrons of VB were excited to CB and then transferred to the d orbit of Fe3+ instead of returning to VB, thereby quenching the fluorescence of MN-CDs through the PET process (Yan et al., 2016; Guo et al., 2018) (Fig. 4f). In total, the synergistic effects of IFE and PET mechanisms yielded the fluorescence quenching of MN-CDs induced by Fe3+.

As vividly elaborated in Fig. S9, the addition of AA led to the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+, among which the hydroxyl groups on the AA molecules were oxidized to ketone groups. This redox reaction hindered the quenching effect induced by Fe3+, but restored the inner fluorescence of MN-CDs (Guo et al., 2019b). Based on the principle that Fe2+ and 1,10-phenanthroline can form orange-red complexes, we added 1,10-phenanthroline to the MN-CDs/Fe3+ system in the presence of AA and other reducing substances. As anticipated, only AA produced the strongest reduction reaction to transfer Fe3+ to Fe2+, yielding bright orange-red color (Fig. S10), which was consistent with the findings of the selectivity experiments described in Section 3.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real application of the “on-off-on” probe for analyses of Fe3+ and AA
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the feasibility of as-constructed “on-off-on” platform, this MN-CDs-based fluorescence probe was employed in real-world samples, including environmental waters, fruit juices and pharmaceutical tablets for the analyses of Fe3+ and AA. As summarized in Table 1, no Fe3+ was detected in tap, drink and river waters, suggesting the concentrations of Fe3+ were all below its detection limit (0.3 μM) in these samples. At three fortification concentrations (10, 20 and 40 μM), the recoveries for Fe3+ were in the range of 92.5–107.0% with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of < 3.2% (n = 3). The intra-day and inter-day precision, expressed as RSDs in six replicates, were in the range of 1.0–3.8% and 1.2–4.6%, respectively. These findings evidence that the MN-CDs-based probe can be used for the sensitive detection of Fe3+ in different water samples.

Similarly, the MN-CDs/Fe3+ system was applied for investigating the practicability for AA detection in peach juice, orange juice, tangerine juice, pear juice, tomato juice and vitamin C tablet samples. As generalized in Table S6 and Table 2, the recoveries for AA varied between 93.7% and 105.1% with the RSDs < 4.7% (n = 3). In the unfortified vitamin C tablet (blank sample), the detected concentration of AA was 27.5 μM. By way of contrast, the concentrations of AA detected in the blank fruit juices changed from 7.2 to 10.6 μM. The intra-day precision in VC tablet and fruit juices was 1.3–3.3% and 1.2–5.0%, while the inter-day precision was 2.2–5.0% and 2.0–5.2%, respectively. In order to further verify the feasibility of this method, AA in VC tablets (1 tablet =100 mg AA) was determined by 2,6-dichlorobenzene oxygen titration according to the China GB.5009.86-2016 method (Li et al., 2012), The blue basic dye, 2,6-dichloroindophenol sodium solution, was used to carry out redox titration on the solution containing AA. When reaching the titration end point, the dye showed light red in the acidic solution, and the content of AA was calculated according to the dye consumption. The color change process is shown in Fig. S11 during the experiment. The detected results showed that the content of AA in each tablet was 98.0 mg/tablet with RSD of 2.1%. In contrast, the as-constructed probe gave the detected value of 98.7 mg/tablet with RSD of 1.1%, yielding a satisfactorily relative recovery of 100.7% compared to the China GB.5009.86–2016 method. As a consequence, it can be posited that the MN-CDs-based switch probe has good accuracy and precision in detecting AA in actual samples.

Interestingly, as vividly elaborated in Fig. S12a and b, our naked eyes could distinguish the color changes from dark blue and bright blue under 365-nm UV lamp when adding different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–30 mM) and AA (0–40 mM) to the test papers. Thus, we posit that the as-developed MN-CDs-based “on-off-on” platform can be used for the visually qualitative detection of Fe3+ and AA with our naked eyes, which is very convenient for outdoor assay by a small/portable UV analyzer encapsulated in a cassette.

For the sake of resource utilization of abandoned Myria rubra in South China, we fabricated, for the first time, the MN-CDs with strong inner fluorescence and good chemical stability. The FIs of MN-CDs were prominently quenched by Fe3+, while they were immediately recovered upon addition of AA, resulting from the redox reaction of AA and Fe3+. Building upon the above principle, the as-prepared MN-CDs were employed to construct an "on-off-on" fluorescence sensor for the sequential detection of Fe3+ and AA in complex matrices. Fluorescence quenching of the MN-CDs induced by Fe3+ was evidenced to be a synergistic mechanism between inner filtering effect and photo-induced electron transfer. The LR and LOD were 1–1000 μM and 0.30 μM, respectively, for Fe3+ in environmental waters, while those are 0.10–1000 μM and 0.03 μM, respectively, for AA in pharmaceutical tablets and fruit juice samples. Based on the visual color changes from dark blue to bright blue, we fabricated test papers, which can be used for outdoor convenient detection of Fe3+ and AA with our naked eyes. Overall, the newly developed MN-CDs-based switch probe has the advantages of short reaction time, wide detection range and outdoor feasibility, and thus shows great potential in real applications.

Ru Fan: Writing – original draft. Jianxing Xiang: Methodology. Peipei Zhou: Formal analysis, Resources. He Mei: Conceptualization. Yanyan Li: Project administration, Resources. Huili Wang: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xiaodong Liu: Funding acquisition. Writing – review & editing. Xuedong Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Data Curation.
